problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations_research / sports analytics): using one season of the "Huskies" soccer team's data (38 games vs 19 opponents, 23,429 passes among 366 players, 59,271 match events), build a team passing network, define indicators that measure teamwork effectiveness, evaluate the network against those indicators, propose concrete coaching strategies to improve Huskies' play, and generalize the findings into a model of effective team cooperation beyond soccer.
models_used: Social Network Analysis (SNA) of the passing network — directed, weighted graph with players as nodes; node position = player's average (x,y) court coordinate; edge weight = number of passes i->j
Weighted adjacency matrix A (a_ij = passes from player i to player j per match) as the core data structure
Network centroid (x0,y0) = pass-weighted average court position

Weighted centroid dispersion Cdw = pass-weighted standard deviation of player distances from centroid (their custom correction of standard centroid dispersion to weight bench players less) — used as aggregation index tp1
Maximum network radius r_m = max distance of any player from centroid — used as 'extension' index tp2
Core/Periphery analysis (via UCINET) to partition players into core vs periphery nodes

Network motif analysis: enumeration of the 13 distinct 3-node directed motif types (borrowed from biology/protein-structure motifs), counting motif frequencies per team per game (via R igraph graph.motifs)
Degree centrality of nodes (per-player pass involvement) for individual-network analysis
Pass timeline / dynamics curve (cumulative passes vs match time)
Multiple linear regression of goal difference on standardized indicators, estimated with Robust (heteroskedasticity-consistent) standard errors in Stata
Coefficient of Variation (CV) of per-player pass counts as a contribution-evenness indicator pi2
Correlation-coefficient screening and VIF (variance inflation factor) multicollinearity check
key_techniques: Three-level decomposition of the network: whole network (centroid, dispersion, radius, adjacency matrix), local network (core/periphery, motifs), individual network (degree centrality)

Engineering 7 candidate features in two families — 3 'performance indicators' (pi1 total passes, pi2 CV of contributions, pi3 midfielders/core-players pass-possession rate) and 4 'team-level processes' (tp1 aggregation=Cdw, tp2 extension=r_m, tp3 tempo=time to first 50 passes, tp4 connected-configuration ratio = freq(motif12+motif13)/total motif freq)
Choosing goal difference (Huskies score minus opponent) as the regression target instead of a win/loss dummy, deliberately to retain more information about outcome margin
Standardizing all variables to unit standard deviation before regression to remove dimensional effects

Two-stage feature screening: scatterplots drop pi2 and tp1 (no visible trend), then regression drops pi1 (counter-intuitive negative sign, treated as spurious correlation) and pi3 (p>0.9), leaving a parsimonious 3-variable model (tp2, tp3, tp4)
Defining the novel 'connected configuration ratio' tp4 from motif 12 and 13 (highest-connectivity 3-node motifs) as a proxy for flexible 3-player passing patterns
Using Robust SEs specifically to guard against heteroskedasticity in cross-sectional game data
Benchmarking Huskies on each surviving indicator against the other 19 teams (rankings out of 20) to turn coefficients into actionable diagnosis

Mapping each surviving statistical indicator to a concrete coaching strategy (tp4->3-player group drills, tp2->physical/conditioning training for court coverage, tp3->avoid fast opening tempo vs strong teams)

Generalizing the four indicators into abstract teamwork principles (frequent interaction, full task coverage/division of labor, gradual pacing, intra-subgroup communication) plus qualitative factors (collective consciousness, cores, trust)
why_it_won: The paper wins on a coherent, original feature-engineering narrative rather than heavy machinery. It takes a borrowed-but-uncommon idea (network motifs from biology) and turns it into a genuinely novel, well-motivated indicator (the connected-configuration ratio tp4), which gives judges something fresh and defensible. Crucially it closes the full ICM loop: it does not stop at descriptive network statistics but feeds engineered indicators into a statistically validated regression (F=13.05, p<0.0001, R^2=0.41, Root MSE~1.5, all three predictors passing t-tests at 95%, VIF confirming no multicollinearity), then translates each surviving, sign-correct coefficient into specific, intuitive coaching advice, and finally abstracts to a general teamwork model as the prompt demanded. The honesty of its modeling — discarding pi1 because its negative coefficient was 'inconsistent with actual significance' and re-examining it via correlation (r=0.32, weak positive) — signals statistical maturity that judges reward.
section_highlights: Strong structural choices: (1) The three-tier network breakdown (whole/local/individual) gives the reader an explicit mental map before any math. (2) Clean separation of indicator DEFINITION (4.3) from indicator SCREENING/TESTING (4.4) makes the modeling pipeline legible. (3) The regression section shows its work transparently — two successive Stata regression tables, the explicit drop logic, and a VIF table — so the parsimonious final model feels earned, not asserted. (4) The Strengths/Weaknesses section is candid and specific (admits ignoring sub-game time scales, pass types like 'Free Kick'/'Duel', and home/away/coach effects). (5) Each strategy subsection (4.6.x) is anchored to a specific indicator and its ranking-vs-20-teams, so advice is data-backed. (6) A real, runnable appendix (MATLAB for adjacency matrix and pass-possession rate, R/igraph for motif frequencies, full standardized regression dataset) makes results reproducible.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Final R^2 is only ~0.41 — the model explains under half the variance in goal difference, so its predictive claim ('can effectively predict the 38 matches') is overstated relative to the fit

Motif enumeration is internally inconsistent: the text and theory describe 13 motifs of SIZE-3 sub-networks, but the appendix R code calls graph.motifs(network, size=4) — a size-4 motif census, a likely error that undermines the headline tp4 indicator

Dropping pi1 (total passes) because its regression coefficient had an 'inconsistent' sign is a post-hoc rationalization; a negative sign more likely reflects confounding/omitted-variable bias and should have prompted a causal-design discussion, not deletion
n=38 games with up to 7 candidate predictors risks overfitting and unstable coefficients before screening; the screening itself was partly eyeballed from scatterplots

Indicators are computed only on SUCCESSFUL passes; intercepts, turnovers, fouls, and pass types are ignored, so the network omits exactly the defensive/transition information that drives many outcomes (the authors acknowledge this)
Home/away, substitutions beyond a crude pass<10 filter, and coaching changes are assumed away, weakening external validity
pi3 'pass-possession rate' relies on a hard-coded midfield definition (avg x in [40,60] in the MATLAB) that is brittle and pitch-orientation-dependent
The generalized teamwork model (4.7) is largely qualitative analogy (collective consciousness, trust) with no validation, so it reads as plausible storytelling rather than a derived result
Several rendered data tables/scatterplots in the source appear corrupted or duplicated (e.g., the tp2 scatter), making some numeric claims hard to audit
reusable_patterns: Two-family feature taxonomy: separate static 'performance indicators' (counts, ratios, dispersion) from dynamic 'process indicators' (tempo, time-to-threshold) — a clean way to organize engineered features for any team/system problem

Borrow a structural primitive from another field (network motifs from molecular biology) and define a single scalar summary statistic from it (ratio of high-connectivity motifs) as your signature original indicator
Use a continuous, information-rich target (goal DIFFERENCE) instead of a binary outcome whenever margin data exists
Standardize-then-screen-then-regress pipeline with explicit, documented drop criteria (scatter trend -> sign sanity -> p-value -> VIF) so reviewers can follow every elimination
Always estimate with robust/heteroskedasticity-consistent SEs for cross-sectional small-n regressions and report VIF to preempt multicollinearity objections
Convert each statistically significant coefficient into one concrete, named recommendation, and justify it with a benchmark ranking of your subject against all peers in the dataset
Three-level network analysis template (whole / local / individual) reusable for any relational/operations dataset
Ship a reproducible appendix with the actual MATLAB/R code and the standardized regression dataset — strong credibility signal to judges

When a predictor has a counter-intuitive sign, follow up with a simple correlation to characterize the raw relationship rather than silently dropping it (here r=0.32 rescued the narrative that passes still matter)
Close ICM problems by abstracting domain-specific results into a domain-general model as an explicit final section, mirroring the prompt's 'generalize' ask
